content:
# Philippians Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 194-195 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_001_page_001.jpg, PHP_001_page_002.jpg

---

1. Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus, to all the saints in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi, with the overseers and deacons:

2. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

3. I give thanks to my God upon every remembrance of you,

4. always in every prayer of mine for you all, making my prayer with joy,

5. because of your fellowship in the gospel from the first day until now,

6. being confident of this very thing, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion until the day of Christ Jesus.

7. It is right for me to think this concerning you all, because I hold you in my heart, both in my chains and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel, you all being fellow partakers with me of grace.

8. For God is my witness, how I long for you all with the affection of Christ Jesus.

9. And this I pray, that your love may abound still more and more in knowledge and all discernment,

10. so that you may approve the things that are excellent, in order that you may be pure and blameless for the day of Christ,

11. having been filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God.

12. Now I want you to know, brothers, that the things concerning me have turned out rather for the advancement of the gospel,

13. so that my chains have become manifest in Christ in the whole praetorium, and to all the rest,

14. and that most of the brothers in the Lord, having been made confident by my chains, are much more bold to speak the word without fear.

15. Some indeed preach Christ even from envy and strife, but some also from good will.

16. The latter do so out of love, knowing that I am appointed for the defense of the gospel;

17. the former proclaim Christ out of selfish ambition, not sincerely, supposing to raise up affliction for me in my chains.

18. What then? Only that in every way, whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is proclaimed, and in this I rejoice. Indeed, I will continue to rejoice,

19. for I know that this will turn out for my deliverance through your prayer and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ,

20. according to my eager expectation and hope, that in nothing I shall be put to shame, but with all boldness, as always, so now also Christ will be magnified in my body, whether through life or through death.

21. For to me, to live is Christ and to die is gain.

22. But if to live in the flesh — if this means fruitful labor for me, then what I shall choose I do not make known.

23. For I am hard pressed between the two, having the desire to depart and to be with Christ, for that is far better;

24. yet to remain in the flesh is more needful on account of you.

25. And being confident of this, I know that I shall remain and continue with you all, for your progress and joy in the faith,

26. so that your boasting may abound in Christ Jesus on account of me, through my coming to you again.

27. Only conduct yourselves in a manner worthy of the gospel of Christ, so that whether I come and see you or am absent, I may hear concerning you that you stand firm in one spirit, with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel,

28. and not being frightened in anything by those who oppose you — which is to them a sign of destruction, but to you of salvation, and this from God —

29. because to you it has been granted on behalf of Christ, not only to believe in him, but also to suffer on his behalf,

30. having the same struggle which you saw in me and now hear to be in me.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript observations (Codex Sinaiticus, pages 194-195):**
The text of Philippians begins in the third column of page 194, following the conclusion of Ephesians. The scriptio continua is written in the characteristic four-column format of Sinaiticus. The chapter continues across page 195, where it concludes partway through the second column. The hand is consistent and legible throughout.

**Key translation decisions:**

1. **"Servants" (v. 1):** Greek ΔΟΥΛΟΙ (douloi). Rendered "servants" rather than "slaves" to preserve the theological dignity of the self-designation while remaining faithful to the semantic range. The term carries the sense of one who belongs wholly to a master.

2. **"Overseers and deacons" (v. 1):** Greek ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΔΙΑΚΟΝΟΙ (episkopoi kai diakonoi). Rendered with functional English equivalents rather than ecclesiastical titles ("bishops") to reflect the early, pre-hierarchical usage visible in the Pauline corpus.

3. **"Fellowship" (v. 5):** Greek ΚΟΙΝΩΝΙΑ (koinonia). Retained as "fellowship" rather than "partnership" to preserve the richer theological overtone of shared participation in the gospel.

4. **"Affection" (v. 8):** Greek ΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΟΙΣ (splanchnois), literally "bowels" or "intestines" — the ancient seat of deep emotion. Rendered "affection" to convey the visceral depth without the anatomical literalism that would distract the English reader.

5. **"Praetorium" (v. 13):** Greek ΠΡΑΙΤΩΡΙΩ (praitorio). Retained as a transliteration, as the term refers to the imperial guard or governor's headquarters and has no single English equivalent that captures both possibilities.

6. **Verses 16-17 — Order:** Sinaiticus preserves the order with love in v. 16 and selfish ambition in v. 17, which reverses the order found in some later manuscripts. This follows the manuscript as read.

7. **"Eager expectation" (v. 20):** Greek ΑΠΟΚΑΡΑΔΟΚΙΑ (apokaradokia) — a rare, vivid compound word suggesting a craning of the neck in anticipation. "Eager expectation" captures both the intensity and the forward-looking posture of the term.

8. **"Conduct yourselves" (v. 27):** Greek ΠΟΛΙΤΕΥΕΣΘΕ (politeuesthe), literally "live as citizens." This is a politically charged term, especially in a letter to a Roman colony. Rendered "conduct yourselves" to preserve the public, civic dimension of the exhortation without over-translating.

9. **"Struggle" (v. 30):** Greek ΑΓΩΝΑ (agona), from which English "agony" derives. Rendered "struggle" rather than "conflict" to maintain the athletic/contest imagery latent in the Pauline original.

**Sinaiticus-specific readings:** The text follows the Sinaiticus readings throughout, including the verse 16-17 order reversal noted above and the reading ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ (Christ Jesus) in v. 1 and throughout, which is characteristic of this manuscript.

— LOGOS
